speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing with Mr. William Cohn, in Dallas, Texas, United States of America. Mr. Cohn, we were just speaking about your position. And you were talking about your colleagues?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, it was the time of first the Civil Rights Movement and then the Vietnam War. And I was-- I became active in the Civil Rights Movement. And I spoke out often in my workplace. You know? And tried to educate them about racism and the injustice of it, and so on. And of course, I talked to deaf ears and only made more enemies. And then when that-- when the Vietnam War was, I became very active-- very active in that, in the peace movement. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And also [INAUDIBLE] then, I had talked against the Vietnam War. At a time when-- when they thought, you know, to-- we have to fight communism.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you have to defend America, you know? And-- and [? I know-- ?] these people didn't see the-- the stupidity and the arrogance of this war. You know? And I always talked-- talked against it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and they-- they even denounced me to the-- I think to the-- to the FBI. And I do have a record on file, in the-- in the FBI office in Chicago. I have a record there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, they eventually-- they eventually succeeded in-- in having me fired.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that information get to the FBI?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- the-- the file, I don't know how far that went from my working place. But the file I have, because I was active in a-- in a group, in an anti-war group, in Chicago. And we-- we went on demonstrations. And we distributed leaflets. And-- and-- and I-- I sent in letters to the editor under my name. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so one letter I sent in-- and in order to-- to have-- have readers say, oh, this bloody Jew. You know? Right? You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had-- I had one of-- one of the friends in the movement, I had to write-- I wrote my name, you know, my name under the article. And then asked him to-- he was a-- he was a minister. And I asked him to write his-- minister-- Reverend so-and-so, also underneath. So that there was not only a Jew writing. So all this together, it ended up in-- in a file in-- in the FBI. I-- I was told that I have-- I never went to that, to-- Afterwards, you know, the Freedom of Information, you could go and look at it. But I never paid attention.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but I was told by-- by some others that I-- that I am there. And also, one time, I got I got a threatening letter. Oh, I think someone stuck it under my-- under our door or something. I don't remember now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I got a note that the cross-- the cross hairs, you know, the cross hairs are already pointed at me. You know? I didn't pay attention. When you left that position, what did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left that position, what did you do next? Did you go back to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I retired. No. I-- I was 60-- I was 61 years old. And I couldn't find a-- find a job anymore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It, you know, there were about 500 [INAUDIBLE] out of work in Chicago at the time. So I-- I didn't have any chance to find another job. And-- so I applied for-- for Social Security. And I got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In looking back over your life, Mr. Cohn, and your different experiences, what kind of message would you like to leave your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like I just said, threat for peace, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the cause for most violence and war is race hatred, and nationalism and chauvinism and religious fanaticism. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whenever there is an injustice committed, we should have the courage to stand up against it. If not, the hatred and injustice will take over, and-- and reap-- reap-- reap its harvest of blood and murder. Like we see still in-- in Rwanda or in Bosnia, or in Cambodia some years ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [BACKGROUND NOISE] I'm not sure if it will ever end. But we should-- we should not become part of it. We should stand up against it. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And that's-- that's the reason why I became active in the Civil Rights Movement and-- and in the peace movement. Because I felt that my-- all my experiences, I would-- I should have an obligation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for your testimony, Mr. Cohn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Cohn, can you tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my maternal grandfather-- grandmother. It was taken in birth place of my mother, where my grandmother had-- had a store. And it was-- I guess it was taken in the beginning of the century.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Minna Schloss. A little bit of the-- of the-- of the feathers on the hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. Well, we're rolling. So why don't you tell us who this is?

words.text: we're rolling. So why don't you

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother, before she got married. And I think it was taken [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] in [INAUDIBLE], in her birth-- birth town, where she was born. And I think that my father somehow saw the picture and fell in love with her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 12, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was taken, I think, in Stendal, in my-- my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Richard. Pronounced in-- in German, it's Richard. Richard. Richard Cohn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this picture to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is a-- a-- a photograph of my father's store shortly about a year or so after-- or a couple of year after-- after he was married to my mother. And it was in a-- in a house which [INAUDIBLE]. I think I talked about it. Yeah. And-- but that store was destroyed in the Crystal Night. And you can see all these picture windows, they were smashed to pieces. And this was a very narrow street. The-- the-- the opposite-- the opposite side of the street is but-- the-- it's about for hardly two vehicles, cars or-- or horse carts, to-- to pass each other by. It's a very narrow street. And before Hitler take-- before Hitler take-- took over, or after, the SR troops, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the storm troopers, marched through our street with torch-- in-- in the evening, with torch lights. And-- and they sang a song. And the song went like this-- when the Jew blood runs from our knives, things will go twice as good. Soldiers, comrades, hang the Jews. Put the [INAUDIBLE] up against the wall. And that-- I still can hear it. We-- we listen to it up on the third floor. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken-- there's a date on the back of it. Must have been 1912, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: --photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this picture, I think, was taken towards the end of 1915. Because I was born in 1915. And looks-- it looks from there like a-- a six or seven-month-old baby. Right? So-- And on the right hand is my father. There's my sister on his lap. In the middle is my grandmother, my father's mother. And on the left side is my mother. And I, on her lap.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. On the left is my father. On the right is my mother. And these pictures were taken for a passport. And it was-- they were taken after to-- my father was released from the concentration camp. And you can recognize that on the-- on his expression.

speaker: interviewer
text: When year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- was in 19-- beginning, probably, of [? 1929. ?] And your parents' names are? Richard-- Richard. And my mother's name is Auguste. Auguste. And her-- her nickname when she was a little child was [GERMAN]. In-- in-- in southern German dialect, [GERMAN] is a telegram. Because she was so quick, they called her the littlest telegram.

speaker: interviewer
text: Photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1945, when my wife and I got married. It's a-- it's a wedding picture. And I was still in party-- in-- in uniform. Because I still was not discharged from the army. Because they couldn't find my papers. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. During the war, the communications between families in enemy countries was taken over by the International Red Cross. So the-- the Red Cross had these kind of-- these cards, where-- where the address, the sender, and the-- the addressee was written on it. And I think that 25 words, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who sent it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A message.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who sent it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this one here, I had four of these cards. And this one here, this was written by my mother before she was deported to Warsaw. And she writes, 'My dears, change of address imminent. As yet still unknown. [? Sannchens ?] father stays here. Be brave, my boy. Stay well. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Goodbye, and keep in loving memory, your kissing Mutti. Auguste Cohn.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- can you describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I retired, I went around Chicago and sketched. And this was a sketch in one of the boat harbors in Chicago, of a-- of a-- of a man that fished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: '81. 1981. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Just quick sketch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is-- is a drawing of my-- of my wife. And she visited me in the hospital after my first open-heart surgery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year is this picture? [? CREW: ?] '78.

speaker: interviewee
text: '78. That's when I was in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is a drawing that I thought could be used as a poster for Amnesty International, where I was a member for-- for quite a number of years. And it is based on a-- on a World War II story in Italy of somebody that was imprisoned by the SS. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here-- actually, it was-- and-- and-- the prisoner-- actually a woman, it was a woman. But I made it a man. And he got a message. Or she got a message in her cell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the message said, coraggio. It means courage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you draw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you draw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1983. And up there, the-- the logo of Amnesty International, is a candle, a burning candle with barbed wire around. And I changed it a little bit. I put a candle in between the bars of a prison cell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe this picture for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I made this, drew this picture, I went to-- to Israel. And on the my way to Israel, I stopped over in Munich. And near Munich was the-- the concentration camp of Dachau. And I visit-- I visited it. It's a memorial now. And in front of the crematorium, where the murdered prisoners were-- were burned, their bodies were burned, there stands this-- this statue now. And I did a quick sketch of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- I think it was '92. I marked it '91. So [BACKGROUND NOISE] it must have been true. And on the base of it, the engraving on the base says in German, and it says, 'To honor the dead and to warn the living.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this photograph was taken in Israel, in March 1996. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it is my older daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Delilah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cohn. She kept her-- she kept her name. Although she married, but she kept her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's-- on the right is my granddaughter, Rebecca. And she is now-- this was a few years-- a couple of years ago. And she is now 12 years old. And on the left is my younger grandson. And his name is Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Michael's last name is? Moreau, Cohn-Moreau. And Rebecca's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ssuffness.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Now, ?] who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older grandson. And his name is David. And he's 18. And he is looking for a college now. And he's-- he wants to become an artist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And David's last name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also Cohn-Moreau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my son-in-law, Michael Ssuffness, my granddaughter, Rebecca Auguste, after her-- after my-- after her great-grandmother, my mother-- my mother. And this is my daughter, Dorit. Dorit Raphaela Ssuffness now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to say about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the main thing I-- I wanted to raise is that I-- I was raised with a constant awareness of the Holocaust. It was-- it permeated our lives. I really wasn't aware until, I think, it was the late '70s-- there was an article that was a precursor to the publication of the book, and it appeared in magazine. And I was really shocked and-- and-- and moved that a lot of the aspects of our life that I attributed to be-- being part of the family of the children of immigrants were very common to children of-- of survivors. I really-- I didn't really know there was that sort of a connection. And it was-- it was pretty-- in a way, pretty upsetting, but pretty revealing as well. I think a lot of the aspects of-- of protectiveness of our parents, perhaps less rebellion in that generation, and-- and-- and a feeling that we had to take care of them and-- and be protective of them, that was pretty-- that was a pretty devastating revelation to realize that that was-- that was all part of that. That it was all part and parcel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has your father's experience had an impact on how you've raised your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't really say that. I-- I don't think it really has. I mean, I think the overall-- my overall upbringing, I'm sure, has had-- has had an impact. But I don't-- I'm aware of-- of everything that happened. And I'm conscious of it. But I, you know, try not to be paranoid. And-- and-- and I tend to be reasonably optimistic, that, you know, that it won't happen now. Although it certainly always-- you know, it would be in the back of my mind. But I've tried to not let-- not let it permeate our lives. I-- I, you know, want my, you know, Rebecca to be aware of what happened. And-- and she has read books. But I don't really want to press it too much. Because I don't think-- I don't think at her age, necessarily, that it's altogether appropriate to know everything. And I was also struck. We went last year to Israel. And we took Michael and Rebecca and I all went together. And we also went with my sister, Delilah, and her sons, David and Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we went to Yad Vashem, the Holocaust center in Jerusalem, and we were, you know, looking at the exhibits, and-- and-- which are very striking. And I was interested to hear my sister's younger son, Michael-- who's just now-- he's 15-- and Rebecca talking that they've heard so much about it all their lives that in some ways, that they're almost shockproof. You know? That they're-- they're somewhat immune.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I feel that sometimes perhaps by overly exposing the kids-- I mean, I think it's important they be aware. But by overly exposing them, I think sometimes you dilute some of the effect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Cohn, is there anything else that you'd like to say, with your family here by you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as immigrants to the United States, both my daughters were born in Israel. So when we came here, Dorit was three and a half. And Delilah was six and a half. And we immigrants, in passing through all the difficulties we had, we became, I think, a very close-knit family unit that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] defensive-- kind of defensive to our environment, our new environment. And that made, for-- I think, for a very close-knit relationship. And unfortunately, my wife did not live to see her own daughters-- being-- well, Dorit-- being happily married-- the other daughter, unfortunately, divorced-- but to see her-- her grandchildren. And it was a regret that I have. But otherwise, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] although I was never very happy here, but my-- my daughters and their families, I'm glad that they-- that they made-- made it here, that they make-- are living and-- and feel-- feel at home here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
